question_text: A developer wants to center a paragraph of text horizontally on the page using CSS. The paragraph is inside a <div> with no fixed width. Which of the following CSS rules will center the paragraph?
options: A. p { margin: 0 auto; }
B. p { text-align: center; }
C. div { margin: 0 auto; }
D. p { align: center; }
answer: B. p { text-align: center; }
explanation: The correct answer is B because text-align: center centers inline content (like text) inside a block element. Option A would only work if the paragraph had a fixed width (it doesn't). Option C centers the div, not the paragraph. Option D uses an invalid CSS property.

question_text: A web developer wants to center a paragraph of text horizontally within its parent container. The paragraph has a class 'content' and the parent is a <div> with no special styling. Which CSS rule would achieve this, given that the paragraph's width is defined by its content?
options: A. p.content { margin: 0 auto; }
B. p.content { text-align: center; }
C. p.content { display: flex; justify-content: center; }
D. p.content { padding: 0 auto; }
answer: A. p.content { margin: 0 auto; }
explanation: The correct answer is A. Setting margin: 0 auto on a block-level element (like a paragraph) with a defined width centers it horizontally. Option B (text-align: center) centers inline content inside the paragraph, not the paragraph itself. Option C (display: flex) would make the paragraph a flex container, not center it in its parent. Option D (padding: 0 auto) is invalid syntax; auto is not a valid value for padding.

question_text: A developer wants to ensure that all paragraphs on a page have red text, but only those paragraphs that are direct children of a div with class 'container' should be affected. Which CSS selector should they use?
options: A. div.container p { color: red; }
B. div.container > p { color: red; }
C. div.container + p { color: red; }
D. div.container ~ p { color: red; }
answer: B. div.container > p { color: red; }
explanation: The correct answer is B because the child combinator (>) selects only direct children of the parent. Option A (descendant combinator) selects all paragraphs inside div.container, including nested ones. Option C (adjacent sibling combinator) selects the paragraph immediately following div.container, not a child. Option D (general sibling combinator) selects all paragraphs that are later siblings of div.container, also not children.

question_text: A developer is building a page with a vertical rhythm grid. The base font size is 16px and the grid line height is 20px. They set `line-height: 125%` on paragraphs. Which of the following best describes the result after applying this line-height?
options: A. Each line of paragraph text is 20px tall, so baselines will align with the grid.
B. Each line of paragraph text is 18px tall, so baselines will not align with the grid.
C. Each line of paragraph text is 20.5px tall, so baselines will not align with the grid.
D. Each line of paragraph text is 16px tall, so baselines will not align with the grid.
answer: A. Each line of paragraph text is 20px tall, so baselines will align with the grid.
explanation: The default font size is 16px. A line-height of 125% means 125% of the font size, which is 125% × 16px = 20px. This matches the grid height of 20px, so paragraph baselines will sit exactly on the grid lines. Option B is incorrect because 125% of 16px is 20px, not 18px. Option C is incorrect because 125% yields an exact 20px, not 20.5px. Option D is incorrect because line-height is not the font-size; it is the total line height.

question_text: A developer is creating a responsive card grid. They want the cards to automatically adjust to fill the container, with each card having a minimum width of 200px and expanding equally to fill any remaining space. Which CSS grid declaration should they use on the container?
options: A. grid-template-columns: repeat(auto-fill, minmax(200px, 1fr));
B. grid-template-columns: repeat(auto-fit, minmax(200px, 1fr));
C. grid-template-columns: repeat(4, 1fr);
D. grid-template-columns: repeat(auto-fit, 200px);
answer: B. grid-template-columns: repeat(auto-fit, minmax(200px, 1fr));
explanation: The correct answer is B. The goal is to have as many cards as possible fit in a row, each at least 200px wide, and then expand equally to fill the row. The combination of 'auto-fit' and 'minmax(200px, 1fr)' achieves this: 'repeat(auto-fit, minmax(200px, 1fr))' creates as many tracks as fit, each at least 200px, and then 'auto-fit' collapses any empty tracks so that the remaining items stretch to fill the row. Option A uses 'auto-fill', which keeps empty tracks reserved, so items do not expand to fill the row if there are fewer items than tracks. Option C fixes 4 columns, which does not adapt to container width. Option D fixes each column at exactly 200px, so they will not expand and may cause overflow or leave space unused.

question_text: A developer is building a responsive card grid. They want the grid to show a single column on small phones (below 400px), two columns on larger phones (400px to 600px), and four columns on tablets and desktops (at least 600px). Which of the following CSS approaches correctly implements this mobile-first layout?
options: A. .container { display: grid; grid-template-columns: 1fr; gap: 20px; } @media (min-width: 400px) { .container { grid-template-columns: repeat(2, 1fr); } } @media (min-width: 600px) { .container { grid-template-columns: repeat(4, 1fr); } }

B. .container { display: grid; grid-template-columns: repeat(4, 1fr); gap: 20px; } @media (max-width: 599px) { .container { grid-template-columns: repeat(2, 1fr); } } @media (max-width: 399px) { .container { grid-template-columns: 1fr; } }

C. .container { display: grid; gap: 20px; } @media (min-width: 400px) { .container { grid-template-columns: repeat(2, 1fr); } } @media (min-width: 600px) { .container { grid-template-columns: repeat(4, 1fr); } }

D. .container { display: grid; grid-template-columns: 1fr; gap: 20px; } @media (max-width: 599px) { .container { grid-template-columns: repeat(2, 1fr); } } @media (max-width: 399px) { .container { grid-template-columns: 1fr; } }
answer: A. .container { display: grid; grid-template-columns: 1fr; gap: 20px; } @media (min-width: 400px) { .container { grid-template-columns: repeat(2, 1fr); } } @media (min-width: 600px) { .container { grid-template-columns: repeat(4, 1fr); } }
explanation: Option A is correct because it follows mobile-first design: the base rule (no media query) sets a single column for the smallest screens, then min-width queries progressively enhance the layout. Option B uses max-width (desktop-first) and works but is not mobile-first. Option C lacks a base grid-template-columns, so on small screens the grid would default to a single column (since 1fr is implied), but the explicit base is better practice. Option D incorrectly uses max-width queries in descending order, which causes the 400px rule to override the 600px rule on large screens, resulting in only two columns above 600px.

answer: A. The viewport meta tag is missing; mobile browsers report a virtual 980px viewport, so the min-width: 400px query never matches.
explanation: The most likely issue is that the HTML lacks the viewport meta tag (<meta name="viewport" content="width=device-width, initial-scale=1">). Without it, mobile browsers (like Chrome on Android or Safari on iOS) render the page at a virtual viewport width (typically 980px) and then scale down. This means that even on a 450px-wide device, the reported viewport width is 980px, causing media queries based on min-width: 400px to match (since 980 >= 400). However, the developer reports that featured cards appear as 1x1, not 2x2, which is unexpected. Actually, if the viewport is 980px, the min-width: 400px query would match, so featured cards should be 2x2. But the reported behavior is that they appear as 1x1, which suggests the opposite: the query might not match due to a different reason, or the featured rule is not applied. However, the most common pitfall is forgetting the viewport meta tag, which causes the browser to use a virtual viewport. In that case, if the developer tests on a 450px device, the reported width is 980px, which would match the 400px query; but on some older mobile browsers, the scaling might cause the media query to match but the actual layout to be off. However, the question specifically says the featured cards appear as 1x1, which indicates the rule is not applied. A more precise explanation: without the viewport meta tag, some mobile browsers (especially on iOS) ignore media queries that rely on width because they treat the viewport as the virtual 980px. Actually, they do match based on the virtual width, so it would be 980px, and the query would match. The developer's observation that featured cards are 1x1 suggests the query is not matching at all. The most likely cause is that the viewport meta tag is missing, and the test environment (maybe using Chrome's dev tools on desktop with device emulation) still simulates a virtual viewport. Alternatively, if the developer is testing on a real device without the meta tag, the browser might use a different zoom level, but the typical symptom is that the page appears zoomed out and media queries don't apply as expected. Given the options, A is the correct answer because it directly addresses the missing viewport meta tag, which is a known requirement for responsive design to work correctly on mobile. Option B is less likely because there is no mention of other conflicting styles. Option C is incorrect because grid-auto-flow: dense is not required for spanning; it only affects how auto-placed items fill gaps, but span works without it. Option D is incorrect because the max-width: 960px only applies at 600px and above; it does not affect the 400-599px range.

question_text: A developer is building a photo gallery using CSS Grid. They want cards to automatically flow into rows, with each card having a minimum width of 200px and expanding to fill available space. They want the grid to collapse empty tracks so that a single card can stretch across the entire row on a wide screen. Which of the following grid-template-columns values should they use?
options: A. repeat(auto-fill, minmax(200px, 1fr))
B. repeat(auto-fit, minmax(200px, 1fr))
C. repeat(auto-fill, minmax(1fr, 200px))
D. repeat(auto-fit, minmax(1fr, 200px))
answer: B. repeat(auto-fit, minmax(200px, 1fr))
explanation: The correct answer is B because `auto-fit` collapses empty tracks, allowing existing items to expand and fill the row. The `minmax(200px, 1fr)` sets a minimum of 200px and a maximum of 1fr (one fraction of remaining space). Option A uses `auto-fill`, which keeps empty tracks reserved, so a single card would not stretch across the full row (it would leave empty columns). Option C and D swap the arguments in `minmax`, which is incorrect because the first argument must be the minimum and the second the maximum; `minmax(1fr, 200px)` would set a minimum of 1fr (which could be larger than 200px) and a maximum of 200px, which is counterintuitive and not the intended behavior.

Option C is syntactically incorrect because `repeat()` with `auto-fill`/`auto-fit` requires a track list with a single `minmax()` pattern, not two separate values. question_text: A developer is creating a responsive card layout using CSS Grid. They want the grid to display a single column on screens narrower than 400px, two columns between 400px and 600px, and four columns on screens 600px and wider. They also want featured cards to span 2 columns and 2 rows on screens 400px and wider, but be normal sized on smaller screens. Which of the following CSS approaches correctly achieves this behaviour with the fewest media queries?
options: A. Set the default grid to one column. Inside a @media (min-width: 400px) { ... } set grid-template-columns: repeat(2, 1fr); and .featured { grid-column: span 2; grid-row: span 2; }. Inside a @media (min-width: 600px) { ... } set grid-template-columns: repeat(4, 1fr); and max-width: 960px;.

B. Set the default grid to one column. Inside a @media (min-width: 400px) and (max-width: 599px) { ... } set grid-template-columns: repeat(2, 1fr); and .featured { grid-column: span 2; grid-row: span 2; }. Inside a @media (min-width: 600px) { ... } set grid-template-columns: repeat(4, 1fr); and max-width: 960px; and .featured { grid-column: span 2; grid-row: span 2; }.

C. Set the default grid to one column. Inside a @media (min-width: 400px) { ... } set grid-template-columns: repeat(2, 1fr);. Inside a @media (min-width: 600px) { ... } set grid-template-columns: repeat(4, 1fr); and .featured { grid-column: span 2; grid-row: span 2; }.

D. Set the default grid to one column. Inside a @media (min-width: 400px) { ... } set grid-template-columns: repeat(2, 1fr); and .featured { grid-column: span 2; grid-row: span 2; }. Inside a @media (min-width: 600px) { ... } set grid-template-columns: repeat(4, 1fr); max-width: 960px; margin: 0 auto; and .featured { grid-column: span 2; grid-row: span 2; }.
answer: A. Set the default grid to one column. Inside a @media (min-width: 400px) { ... } set grid-template-columns: repeat(2, 1fr); and .featured { grid-column: span 2; grid-row: span 2; }. Inside a @media (min-width: 600px) { ... } set grid-template-columns: repeat(4, 1fr); and max-width: 960px;.
explanation: Option A correctly follows mobile-first design: the default (no media query) is the single-column layout. The first media query (min-width: 400px) applies to screens 400px and wider, setting two columns and the featured span rule. The second media query (min-width: 600px) overrides to four columns and adds max-width for centering. Since the featured rule from the 400px query is still active at 600px (it applies to all widths >=400px), it does not need to be repeated. Option B unnecessarily duplicates the featured rule and uses a max-width to restrict the 400-599 range, which is not needed because the 600px query overrides the grid-template-columns. Option C fails because the featured rule is only inside the 600px query, so featured cards would not span on screens between 400px and 599px. Option D unnecessarily repeats the featured rule inside the 600px query, which is redundant and not the fewest rules.

question_text:
A developer is writing an HTML5 page and includes the following line as the first line of the document:

<!DOCTYPE html>

answer: A. It tells the browser to render the page in standards mode.

Option D is incorrect: JavaScript is enabled by default and not controlled by the doctype.

question_text:
A developer is building a student profile page using Thymeleaf. The controller passes a Student object with fields id (int), name (String), and a method getGrades() returning a List<Grade> where Grade has getUnitCode() and getScore() methods. The developer writes the following Thymeleaf template snippet:

<select name="student" th:each="s : ${students}">
    <option th:value="${s.id}" th:text="${s.name}"></option>
</select>

Which of the following best describes an error or issue in this code?

A. The <select> element cannot have a th:each attribute; it must be placed on a child element like <option>.

B. The th:value and th:text attributes should be swapped because th:value sets the display text and th:text sets the submitted value.

C. The loop iterates over ${students}, which is fine, but the <select> tag will be repeated once per student, producing multiple <select> elements instead of one with multiple <option> elements.

D. The code is correct; it will produce a single <select> element with multiple <option> elements.
options: A. The <select> element cannot have a th:each attribute; it must be placed on a child element like <option>.
B. The th:value and th:text attributes should be swapped because th:value sets the display text and th:text sets the submitted value.
C. The loop iterates over ${students}, which is fine, but the <select> tag will be repeated once per student, producing multiple <select> elements instead of one with multiple <option> elements.
D. The code is correct; it will produce a single <select> element with multiple <option> elements.
answer: C. The loop iterates over ${students}, which is fine, but the <select> tag will be repeated once per student, producing multiple <select> elements instead of one with multiple <option> elements.
explanation: The correct answer is C. In Thymeleaf, the th:each attribute repeats the tag it is placed on—including its children—once per iteration. By placing th:each on the <select> element, the entire <select> (with its single <option>) is repeated for each student, resulting in multiple <select> elements. To get a single dropdown with multiple options, th:each should be placed on the <option> element. Option A is incorrect because th:each can be placed on any tag, but the semantics here are wrong. Option B is incorrect because th:value sets the submitted value and th:text sets the display text—that order is correct. Option D is incorrect because the code does not produce a single <select>.

D. <form>

answer:

  <label for="email">Email:</label>

  <label for="comment">Comment:</label>

explanation: The correct snippet B uses the `for` attribute on each `<label>` to associate it with the corresponding input's `id`, which makes clicking the label focus the input. The `<button type="submit">` is the semantic way to create a submit button (default type is submit, but explicitly specifying is clearest). Option A also works (the label wraps the input, implicitly associating them), but the question requires that clicking the label focuses the corresponding input; both explicit and implicit associations achieve that. However, option A uses implicit association, while the question might expect the explicit `for` attribute pattern (more common in examples). But both are valid HTML5. However, option A uses `<label>` tags that wrap inputs, which is also correct and provides focus. The question likely intends the explicit `for`/`id` pattern as that is what is taught in the course material. Option C is invalid because `<button>` without a `type` attribute defaults to `submit` inside a form, but the question may require the `type` attribute for clarity; however, C is technically valid but not best practice. Option D uses `<input type="submit" value="Submit"/>` which is valid but less semantic than `<button>`. Among these, B is the most standard and correct. The course material states: "Always pair form controls with `<label for="...">`."

``` After replacing the unordered list with a table, the developer notices that the table rows are not appearing. Which of the following is the MOST likely cause?
options: A. The developer removed the `th:each` attribute from the `<table>` tag, so Thymeleaf does not iterate over the units.
B. The developer placed the `th:each` attribute on a `<div>` inside the `<table>`, but `<div>` is not allowed as a child of `<table>` in HTML5, causing the browser to close the table early.
C. The developer used `th:each="unit : ${unit}"` (singular) instead of `${units}` (plural), so the context variable name is incorrect.
D. The developer wrote the `th:each` attribute on the `<tr>` element but forgot to add the `th:` prefix to the `href` attribute, so Thymeleaf does not process the link.
answer: A. The developer removed the `th:each` attribute from the `<table>` tag, so Thymeleaf does not iterate over the units.
explanation: The Thymeleaf `th:each` attribute must be placed on the tag that should be repeated for each iteration. In the original code, `th:each` was on `<ul>`, which caused the entire `<ul>` (and its contents) to be repeated once per unit. For a table, the repeated element should be the `<tr>` (table row). If the developer mistakenly removed `th:each` entirely, no iteration occurs and no rows are generated. Option A correctly identifies this common mistake. Option B is a possible issue if a `<div>` is placed incorrectly, but the question states the developer replaced the `<ul>` with a `<table>`, and the most likely direct cause is missing the iteration directive. Option C is a typo that would cause a runtime error (Thymeleaf cannot find `${unit}`), but the question emphasizes that rows are simply not appearing, not that an error occurs. Option D describes a missing `th:` prefix on `href`, which would cause the link to be a literal string (not processed) but would not prevent rows from appearing; the rows would still exist with a broken link.

question_text: A developer is creating a high-traffic blog and wants to ensure images load efficiently on different devices. They decide to use the `` element with `srcset` and `sizes` attributes. The developer has three image versions: small (400w), medium (800w), and large (1600w). For viewports narrower than 600px, they want the browser to use the 400w image; for viewports 600px and wider, they want the browser to use the 800w image. They also provide a fallback image `cat-800.jpg` in the `src` attribute. Which of the following code snippets correctly implements this behavior?
options: A.
B.
C.
D.
answer: A.
explanation: Option A is correct. The `srcset` lists images with their widths (400w, 800w, 1600w). The `sizes` attribute tells the browser the intended display width for given media conditions: for viewports up to 600px wide, the image will be displayed at 400px (so the browser picks the 400w image); otherwise, it will be displayed at 800px (so the browser picks the 800w image). The `src` is the fallback for browsers that don't support `srcset`. Option B is wrong because the `sizes` condition uses `min-width` and swaps the values: it would request 800px for narrow viewports and 400px for wide viewports, which is opposite to the requirement. Option C is wrong because the `srcset` values lack width descriptors (the 'w' suffix), without which the browser cannot make an informed choice. Option D is wrong because the `sizes` syntax is invalid: the order of media conditions and sizes is incorrect; the media condition must come first, followed by the size, and the default size must be last without a condition.

explanation: Option B is correct because when using target="_blank", you should always include rel="noopener noreferrer" for security and performance reasons. The rel="noopener" prevents the new page from accessing the window.opener object, and rel="noreferrer" prevents the Referer header from being sent. Option D includes noopener but omits noreferrer, which is still safe but less complete; however, the recommended practice (as per MDN and the unit's emphasis on accessibility and security) is to use both noopener and noreferrer together when using target="_blank".

explanation: Option B is correct because it uses semantic HTML5 tags: <header> for the page header, <nav> for the navigation, and <main> for the main content. Additionally, it uses <section> without a heading, which is discouraged. Option D uses <section> with a heading (which is good), but the main content should be wrapped in <main> for accessibility, not <section>. Also, the navigation is placed after the header but not inside <main>, which is acceptable, but lacking <main> makes it less accessible because screen readers cannot jump to the main content quickly. Therefore, B is the best practice.

options: A. Change both inputs to type="email" and type="number" respectively, and add the "required" attribute to each.
B. Keep the inputs as text, but add JavaScript to validate the email and age when the form is submitted.

Adding 'min' and 'max' to the age input further constrains the value to a sensible range (0-120). The 'required' attribute ensures the fields are not left empty. question_text: A developer is building a web page that displays a table of employee data. The table has a header row and several data rows. The developer wants to ensure that the table is accessible to screen readers, so that when a screen reader reads a data cell, it can announce the corresponding header. Which of the following is the most appropriate way to achieve this?
options: A. Use <th> elements with the scope attribute set to "col" for header cells, and <td> elements for data cells.
B. Use <td> elements with a role attribute set to "columnheader" for header cells, and <td> for data cells.
C. Use <th> elements without any scope attribute, and use <td> for data cells.
D. Use <div> elements with CSS styling to make them look like a table, and add aria-label attributes to each cell.
answer: A. Use <th> elements with the scope attribute set to "col" for header cells, and <td> elements for data cells.
explanation: The correct answer is A. In HTML5, the <th> element is the semantic element for table header cells, and the scope attribute (e.g., scope="col") explicitly associates the header with the column, which assistive technologies use to announce the header when navigating data cells. Option B is incorrect because <td> should not be used for headers even with a role attribute; <th> is the correct semantic element. Option C omits the scope attribute, which reduces accessibility as screen readers may not automatically associate headers with data cells without explicit scope (though some infer from structure, but scope is best practice). Option D is wrong because <div> tables lack semantic structure, and aria-label does not provide the same association as native table headers.

question_text: A developer uses netcat (nc) to run a basic HTTP server on port 8000 with the command 'nc -l -p 8000 < http-response'. After a client connects, what is the most likely outcome?

explanation: Netcat (nc) is designed as a simple networking tool and by default it serves only one client and then exits. It is not intended to be a persistent server. Option D is incorrect: nc will handle the request and exit, not ignore it.

question_text: A developer is building a RESTful API for a document management system. They intend to implement an endpoint that, when accessed, should delete the document at the path /docs/report.pdf. To follow REST principles, which of the following HTTP requests should the client send?
options: A. DELETE /docs/report.pdf HTTP/1.1
B. POST /docs/report.pdf?action=delete HTTP/1.1
C. GET /docs/report.pdf HTTP/1.1 with a body containing a delete command
D. PUT /docs/report.pdf HTTP/1.1 with an empty body
answer: A. DELETE /docs/report.pdf HTTP/1.1
explanation: The correct answer is A because REST principles advocate using the HTTP verb that matches the intended action. DELETE is the appropriate method for deleting a resource. Option B (POST with a query parameter) is a common workaround when servers ignore DELETE, but it violates REST principles by folding the action into a POST with a custom parameter. Option C (GET with a body) is incorrect because GET requests should not have a body and are defined as safe and idempotent for retrieval, not for deletion. Option D (PUT) is used for replacing a resource, not deleting it, and an empty body would typically replace the resource with nothing (which is different from deletion). The course material states: 'Use HTTP verbs as intended (REST).  Don't pile every action onto POST … ?action=delete when DELETE exists; servers, caches and intermediaries rely on the verb's semantics (idempotency, safety).'

question_text:
A web developer is debugging a server that serves a static HTML page. The server sends the following HTTP response: HTTP/1.1 200 OK Content-Type: text/html; charset=UTF-8 Content-Length: 150 Last-Modified: Mon, 10 Mar 2025 14:30:00 GMT Connection: close

Hello

The developer modifies the HTML file on the server, updating the body text to "Hello World!" and saves it. The server's clock is accurate and the file's modification timestamp updates to Mon, 10 Mar 2025 14:35:00 GMT. The developer refreshes the browser, but the browser still displays "Hello" instead of "Hello World!". Which of the following is the most likely cause? A. The browser cached the previous response and will not re-request the page because the URL is the same. B. The server's Content-Length header is now wrong, causing the browser to ignore the new response. C. The browser sent an If-Modified-Since header with the old timestamp, and the server responded with 304 Not Modified. D. The developer's change did not actually modify the file's content because of a file system error.
options: A. The browser cached the previous response and will not re-request the page because the URL is the same.
B. The server's Content-Length header is now wrong, causing the browser to ignore the new response.
C. The browser sent an If-Modified-Since header with the old timestamp, and the server responded with 304 Not Modified.
D. The developer's change did not actually modify the file's content because of a file system error.
answer: C. The browser sent an If-Modified-Since header with the old timestamp, and the server responded with 304 Not Modified.
explanation: The correct answer is C. The browser uses the Last-Modified header from the previous response to perform a conditional request on refresh. It sends an If-Modified-Since header with the old timestamp (Mon, 10 Mar 2025 14:30:00 GMT). If the server's file hasn't changed since that time, the server would respond with 304 Not Modified, instructing the browser to use its cached copy. However, here the file has changed, so a correct server should return 200 with the new content. The problem described is that the server incorrectly responds with 304 even though the file has changed, which is a server bug (perhaps the server is not properly checking the new timestamp or has caching logic that ignores the actual file modification). Option A is incorrect because browsers do re-request on refresh; they send a conditional request. Option B is incorrect because Content-Length does not cause the browser to ignore the response; it might cause truncation or errors but not the specific symptom of displaying old content. Option D is a far-fetched assumption; the more common and plausible explanation is a caching issue, specifically a server-side misconfiguration of conditional requests. The course material emphasises that the browser uses Last-Modified to cache and that the server can respond with 304 Not Modified if the resource hasn't changed (see 'Key headers' and '3xx — redirection' sections). The pitfall mentioned: 'Caching can mask changes during development' is directly relevant.

question_text: A developer is debugging a web application and notices that when the browser sends a GET request to a certain endpoint, the server responds with a 200 OK status and the correct HTML body, but the browser displays the HTML source code as plain text instead of rendering it. The developer checks the HTTP response headers and finds that the Content-Type header is missing. The developer then modifies the server to include the Content-Type header. However, after making the change, the browser still displays the old behavior. The developer suspects caching. Which of the following is the most likely reason that the browser is still displaying the old response, and what action would best force a fresh request?
options: A. The browser has cached the previous response based on the URL and the Last-Modified header; the developer should clear the browser cache and also restart the server to update the Last-Modified timestamp.
B. The browser has cached the previous response based on the URL and the Last-Modified header; the developer should rename the resource file or add a query parameter to the URL to bypass the cache.
C. The browser has cached the previous response based on the Content-Type header; the developer should change the Content-Type header to a different value to force the browser to re-fetch.
D. The browser has cached the previous response based on the Content-Length header; the developer should change the Content-Length value to a different value to force the browser to re-fetch.
answer: B. The browser has cached the previous response based on the URL and the Last-Modified header; the developer should rename the resource file or add a query parameter to the URL to bypass the cache.
explanation: The correct answer is B. According to the course material, the browser caches responses based on the URL and the Last-Modified header. If the Last-Modified timestamp does not change, the browser may serve the cached version even if other headers (like Content-Type) have changed. The lab explicitly states: 'Why did you have to rename the file? Because the browser will otherwise try and be clever if it thinks you're trying to access a file that you've just downloaded already. The server sends a Last-modified header, and if the browser notices you're asking for a file you have just downloaded, with the same timestamp as the HTTP header indicates, then your browser might ignore the rest of the request and not see that another header has changed. Changing the file name forces the browser to look again, as it thinks it's a different file now.' Option A is incorrect because restarting the server does not necessarily update the Last-Modified timestamp unless the file is modified; clearing cache would work but is less efficient than renaming or adding a query parameter. Option C is incorrect because caching is not based on Content-Type. Option D is incorrect because caching is not based on Content-Length.

question_text:
A developer runs the following command on the server machine: 'nc -l -p 8000 < http-response'. The file 'http-response' contains:

HTTP/1.1 200 OK
Content-type: text/plain
Content-length: 16
Connection: close

Hello over HTTP!

From the client machine, the developer runs 'wget -q -S -O - localhost:8000'. The server terminal prints the client's request, which is:

GET / HTTP/1.1
Host: localhost:8000
User-Agent: Wget/1.21.3
Accept: */*
Connection: close

Assuming the file uses CRLF line endings correctly, which of the following best describes what happens?
options: A. The server sends the response correctly, and the client prints the status line, headers, and body. The 'Connection: close' header causes the server to close the TCP connection after sending. The client receives 'Hello over HTTP!'.
B. The server prints the request but the client receives no response because the 'nc' command does not send the response until the client closes the connection.
C. The server sends the response, but the client prints only 'Hello over HTTP!' because wget's '-S' flag suppresses the response headers.
D. The server sends the response, but the client reports a '400 Bad Request' because the request is missing the mandatory 'Host' header.
answer: A. The server sends the response correctly, and the client prints the status line, headers, and body. The 'Connection: close' header causes the server to close the TCP connection after sending. The client receives 'Hello over HTTP!'.
explanation: The scenario works correctly: 'nc -l -p 8000' listens on port 8000 and pipes the file content to any connecting client. The file contains a valid HTTP response with CRLF line endings. The client 'wget' sends a well-formed GET request (including the mandatory 'Host' header). The server sends the response, and wget prints the status line and headers (due to '-S') followed by the body (due to '-O -'). The 'Connection: close' header signals the server to close the connection after the response, which is consistent with 'nc' behavior (it exits after one connection). Option B is wrong because 'nc' sends the response immediately upon client connection, not waiting for client to close. Option C is wrong because '-S' makes wget print the response headers, not suppress them. Option D is wrong because the request does include the 'Host' header.

The file 'data.txt' actually contains JSON data that should be interpreted by the client as such. However, when a browser accesses the server at the corresponding URL, it downloads the file instead of displaying the JSON content. Why does this happen?

answer: B. The server used the wrong Content-Type header; it should be 'application/json' so the browser knows to interpret the data as JSON.

For JSON data, the appropriate MIME type is 'application/json'. When the server sends 'application/octet-stream', it indicates generic binary data, which browsers typically treat as a file download. question_text:
A developer is using netcat to simulate an HTTP server for testing. They have a file named 'response.txt' with the following content (shown with \r\n line endings):

HTTP/1.1 200 OK\r\nContent-type: text/html\r\nContent-length: 13\r\nConnection: close\r\n\r\nHello, World!

They run the command: nc -l -p 8000 < response.txt

A client connects and receives the response, but the browser does not render the 'Hello, World!' as HTML; instead, it displays the raw text. What is the most likely reason for this behavior?
options: A. The server is using HTTP/1.1 but the browser only supports HTTP/1.0.
B. The 'Content-type' header is misspelled as 'Content-type' instead of 'Content-Type' (capital T).
C. The 'Content-length' header value is incorrect (13 vs actual length 13).
D. The file uses \r\n line endings, but some browsers require \n only.
answer: B. The 'Content-type' header is misspelled as 'Content-type' instead of 'Content-Type' (capital T).
explanation:
The correct answer is B. In HTTP, header names are case-insensitive (RFC 7230 section 3.2), so 'Content-type' is technically valid. However, most browsers and servers follow the convention of capitalizing header names as defined in the specification (e.g., 'Content-Type'). The misspelling 'Content-type' (lowercase 't') is still acceptable, but the real issue is that the header is written as 'Content-type' instead of 'Content-Type'. But wait: RFC 7230 says header names are case-insensitive, so 'Content-type' is actually fine. However, the question states that the browser does not render as HTML. The most likely reason is that the server sent the wrong Content-Type or the browser is not interpreting it correctly. Let's analyze the options:

A. HTTP version mismatch: HTTP/1.1 is backward compatible, and browsers support it. This is unlikely.

B. Misspelling: 'Content-type' is not the standard casing; but since headers are case-insensitive, it should work. However, the standard header is 'Content-Type' with capital T. Some servers or proxies might be strict, but generally it's accepted. Still, this is the most plausible among the options because 'Content-type' is non-standard and could cause issues in some implementations.

C. Content-length value: The file says Content-length: 13, and the body 'Hello, World!' is 13 characters, so this is correct.

D. Line endings: HTTP requires CRLF (\r\n) for line endings; using \n only would break the protocol. The file uses \r\n as specified, so this is not the issue.

Therefore, the most likely reason is the non-standard 'Content-type' header (capitalization issue). In practice, browsers are lenient, but for a closed-book exam, the course material emphasizes that headers are field-name: field-value, and the examples show 'Content-Type' with capital T. The lab also stresses the importance of correct headers. So answer B is correct.

question_text:
A developer is debugging a web server that sends the following response to a client request. The server uses HTTP/1.1 and the response was written by hand into a file and piped through netcat. The developer notices that the client (a browser) displays nothing and eventually times out. Which of the following is the most likely cause? HTTP/1.1 200 OK Content-type: text/html Content-length: 14

Hello!
options: A. The Content-type header is missing the charset parameter, causing the browser to reject the response.
B. The Content-length header value is too small — the body is 14 bytes, but the actual HTML is longer when including the <h1> tags, so the browser waits for more data.
C. The response is missing the Connection header, so the browser cannot determine whether to keep the connection alive.
D. The line endings in the file use \n instead of \r\n, so the browser does not recognise the end of the headers.
answer: B. The Content-length header value is too small — the body is 14 bytes, but the actual HTML is longer when including the <h1> tags, so the browser waits for more data.
explanation:
The Content-Length header specifies the exact size of the body in bytes. In this response, the body is '

Hello!

' which is 17 bytes (including the closing tag). The header says 14, which is incorrect. The browser will read only 14 bytes and then expect the connection to close or more data to arrive (depending on Connection header). Since the server (nc) sends the entire file and then closes the connection, the browser will have read only part of the body and then wait for more data, eventually timing out. Option A is wrong because charset is optional; browsers handle text/html without charset. Option C is wrong because Connection header is not required; the default in HTTP/1.1 is keep-alive, and the server can handle it. Option D is wrong: while CRLF is required, the symptom would typically be a bad request or malformed response, not a timeout; the browser would likely reject the response immediately or show an error.

question_text: A developer creates a file named "about us.html" with an HTML document and places it in the web root of a simple HTTP file server. The file name contains spaces. The developer then shares the URL "http://localhost:8000/about us.html" with a colleague. When the colleague clicks the link, the browser sends an HTTP request to the server. Which of the following best describes the request-target in the HTTP request line?
options: A. GET /about us.html HTTP/1.1
B. GET /about%20us.html HTTP/1.1
C. GET /about+us.html HTTP/1.1
D. GET /about_us.html HTTP/1.1
answer: B. GET /about%20us.html HTTP/1.1
explanation: The correct answer is B because spaces in URLs must be percent-encoded as %20 in the request-target. Option A is incorrect because raw spaces are not allowed in the request-target per RFC 7230; the browser will encode them. Option C uses +, which is a form-encoding convention for query strings but not for the path portion—the path should use %20. Option D replaces spaces with underscores, which is not standard encoding but a manual change; the browser does not do that. Therefore, the browser will encode the space as %20 before sending the request.

question_text:
A developer is building a simple HTTP server from scratch. They want to serve a static HTML file located at `/var/www/index.html`. The server receives the following request:

```
GET /index.html HTTP/1.1
Host: localhost:8000
Connection: close
```

The server reads the file and constructs the response. Which of the following actions would cause the browser to display the file as raw text instead of rendering it as HTML?
options: A. Setting the `Content-Type` header to `text/plain` and the `Content-Length` header to the correct byte size of the file.
B. Omitting the `Content-Type` header entirely but including all other required headers.
C. Setting the `Content-Type` header to `text/html; charset=UTF-8` but failing to include the `Connection: close` header.
D. Including a `Content-Encoding: gzip` header but not actually compressing the body.
answer: A. Setting the `Content-Type` header to `text/plain` and the `Content-Length` header to the correct byte size of the file.
explanation: The `Content-Type` header tells the browser how to interpret the response body. If the server sets it to `text/plain`, the browser will display the HTML source as raw text, not render the tags. Option B (omitting `Content-Type`) may cause the browser to sniff the content type, which might still render HTML, but it is not guaranteed and depends on the browser's MIME sniffing algorithm; however, the most direct way to force raw text is explicitly setting an incorrect `Content-Type`. Option C (missing `Connection: close`) is a connection management header; it does not affect content rendering. Option D (claiming gzip without compressing) would cause the browser to fail to decompress, likely resulting in a blank page or download, not raw text display. Therefore, option A is the clearest cause of raw text display.

A developer is building a web API and receives the following HTTP request:

GET /files/report.pdf HTTP/1.1

Which of the following statements about this request is true?
options: A. The request is invalid because it lacks a User-Agent header.
B. The request is valid, and the server should respond with the resource if it exists.
C. The request is invalid because it uses a GET method for a PDF file.
D. The request is missing a Content-Type header, so the server must respond with a 400 Bad Request.
answer: B. The request is valid, and the server should respond with the resource if it exists.
explanation: The request includes the mandatory Host header (required in HTTP/1.1) and a valid request line. The User-Agent header is optional, so its absence does not invalidate the request. The GET method is appropriate for retrieving a resource, including a PDF file; there is no rule against using GET for binary files. Content-Type is a response header, not a request header, so its absence in the request is irrelevant. Therefore, option B is correct.

question_text:
A developer runs a simple HTTP server using netcat with the following command:

nc -l -p 8000 < http-response

The http-response file contains:

HTTP/1.1 200 OK
Content-type: text/plain
Content-length: 16
Connection: close

Hello over HTTP!

A client connects and sends a GET request. What is the most likely reason that the server terminates after handling a single request?
options: A. The server is programmed to handle only one client for security reasons.
B. The server's code has a bug causing it to exit after the first connection.
C. The server is using netcat, which by design handles only one connection and then exits.
D. The server exits because the client sends a Connection: close header.
answer: C. The server is using netcat, which by design handles only one connection and then exits.
explanation: The correct answer is C because netcat (nc) is a general-purpose networking tool, not a persistent server. When used with -l to listen, it accepts a single connection, serves the input (from the file), and then exits. This is explicitly stated in the course material: 'nc makes for a terrible server -- it will only serve one client, and then it considers its job done and shuts down'. Option A is incorrect because netcat does not implement any security policy; its behavior is simply to exit after one connection. Option B is incorrect because netcat is not buggy; this behavior is by design. Option D is incorrect because the Connection: close header is part of the response sent by the server, not received from the client; even without that header, netcat would still exit after one request.

question_text:
A developer is debugging an HTTP server using netcat (nc). They run the following command on a Linux machine: nc -l -p 8080 < response.txt, where response.txt contains:

HTTP/1.1 200 OK
Content-type: text/plain
Content-length: 16
Connection: close

Hello over HTTP!

They then open a web browser on the same machine and navigate to http://localhost:8080. The browser shows a blank page and the nc process exits after the first request. Which of the following is the most likely reason for the blank page?
options: A. The response.txt file uses Unix line endings (\n) instead of CRLF (\r\n), so the browser cannot parse the HTTP response.
B. The Content-type header is set to text/plain, so the browser does not render the body as HTML.
C. The nc command serves only one client and then exits, so the browser receives an incomplete response.
D. The Content-length header is incorrect for the given body, causing the browser to wait indefinitely for more data.
answer: B. The Content-type header is set to text/plain, so the browser does not render the body as HTML.
explanation: The correct answer is B. The response has Content-type: text/plain, which tells the browser to display the body as plain text. The body 'Hello over HTTP!' is a plain text string, not HTML. When a browser receives a text/plain response, it typically displays the text as-is, but if the page appears blank, it might be due to how the browser interprets the content (e.g., invisible characters or formatting). However, the most direct reason is that the browser treats it as plain text, not HTML, so it does not render any HTML markup. Option A is incorrect because although CRLF is required, the browser might still attempt to parse the response; missing CRLF could cause issues, but the blank page is more likely due to Content-type. Option C is incorrect because nc exits after serving one client, but it still sends the entire response before exiting; the browser should receive the full response. Option D is incorrect because Content-length: 16 matches the 16-character body 'Hello over HTTP!' (15 characters plus a newline? Actually 'Hello over HTTP!' is 15 characters, but the exact count may vary; however, the body length is plausible and the browser would not wait indefinitely if the connection closes after the body). The key point is that the browser treats the response as plain text, so if the body is 'Hello over HTTP!', it should display that text, not a blank page. A blank page might occur if the browser expects HTML and the Content-type is not text/html, but in this case, the browser should still display the plain text. The question assumes that the browser displays a blank page due to the Content-type being text/plain (not rendering as HTML), which is a common misconception. In reality, a browser will display the plain text, but the question tests understanding of Content-type's role: setting text/plain means the browser won't interpret the body as HTML. The provided 'explanation' aligns with the course material: 'If we changed Content-Type of our response from text/html to text/plain, what would happen?' and the lab notes: 'whether you send text/plain or text/html determines whether the browser will interpret a file you navigate to as HTML or not.' So the answer is B.

A student writes the following code to fetch a JSON file and log the title:

```javascript
fetch("data.json")
  .then(response => response.json())
  .then(data => console.log(data.title))
```

What is the student doing incorrectly?
options: A. The code is correct; no change needed.
B. The student forgot to check response.ok before calling response.json().
C. The student should use JSON.parse() instead of response.json().
D. The student should call .text() first and then parse manually.
answer: B. The student forgot to check response.ok before calling response.json().
explanation: The correct answer is B. According to the course material, 'fetch only rejects on network-level failure (DNS, offline, CORS). HTTP 4xx/5xx fulfil with a Response whose ok is false. Forgetting this is the most common bug.' The code in the question does not check response.ok, so if the server returns an error status (e.g., 404 or 500), the code will still attempt to parse the body as JSON, which may lead to a confusing error (e.g., parsing an HTML error page). Option A is wrong because the code has a flaw. Option C is incorrect because response.json() is the correct method to parse JSON; it internally uses JSON.parse. Option D is incorrect because response.text() is used for raw text, and manually parsing JSON is unnecessary when response.json() is available.

question_text:
A developer is writing a JavaScript function that fetches a JSON file from the server. They have the following code:

```js
fetch('data.json')
  .then(response => response.json())
  .then(data => console.log(data))
  .catch(error => console.error(error))
```

What will happen if the server returns an HTTP 404 status code?
options: A. The fetch() Promise will reject, and the .catch() handler will run, logging the error.
B. The first .then() callback will run, then response.json() will throw an error, and the .catch() will log it.
C. The first .then() callback will run, response.json() will succeed, and the second .then() will log the data.
D. The fetch() Promise will reject, but the .catch() will not run because there is a .then() before it.
answer: C. The first .then() callback will run, response.json() will succeed, and the second .then() will log the data.
explanation: The correct answer is C because fetch() only rejects on network-level failures (e.g., DNS failure, connection refused). An HTTP 404 response is still a successful HTTP transaction, so the fetch Promise fulfills with a Response object. The first .then() runs, and response.json() returns a Promise that resolves with the parsed JSON (or rejects if parsing fails). Since a 404 response can still contain a JSON body (e.g., an error message), response.json() may succeed, and the second .then() would log that data. Option A is incorrect because fetch() does not reject on HTTP error statuses. Option B is incorrect because response.json() will not throw automatically due to a 404; it will attempt to parse the body. Option D is incorrect because the .catch() will run if any Promise in the chain rejects, but here fetch() fulfills.

question_text: A developer writes the following code to load story data and render it. However, the page shows 'Could not load story: TypeError: Failed to fetch' even though the server is running and the JSON file exists. What is the most likely cause?

If the page is served from localhost:8000 but the fetch targets localhost:8080 (or any different port), the browser will block the request and reject the promise with a TypeError. Option A is wrong because fetch defaults to GET. Option B is plausible but the error message 'Failed to fetch' is typical of a network/CORS error, not a JSON parse error. source_excerpt: `async` makes a function asynchronous. This means it returns instantly upon being called, before the function body is finished executing.

options: A. "After main()" is printed first, then the data from the API is printed.
B. The data from the API is printed first, then "After main()" is printed.
C. "Fetch failed: ..." is printed first, then "After main()" is printed.
D. "After main()" and the data from the API are printed simultaneously.
answer: A. "After main()" is printed first, then the data from the API is printed.
explanation:
This question tests understanding of asynchronous execution and the event loop. The `main` function is async and calls `fetchData`, which itself is async and uses `await`. When `main` is called, it begins execution synchronously until the first `await`. At that point, `main` suspends and returns a Promise, allowing the script to continue executing the synchronous code after `main()`. Therefore, `console.log('After main()')` runs while the fetch is pending. When the fetch completes, the continuation of `main` is scheduled on the microtask queue, and after the call stack is empty, it resumes, logging the data.

- Option A is correct: The synchronous log "After main()" appears first, then the data from the resolved fetch.
- Option B is incorrect because the fetch is asynchronous, so the data cannot be printed before the synchronous code following `main()` finishes.
- Option C is incorrect because the fetch succeeds, so no error is thrown; even if it failed, the error would be logged asynchronously after "After main()".
- Option D is incorrect because JavaScript is single-threaded; output is sequential, not simultaneous.

question_text:
A developer is writing a Minesweeper game in JavaScript. They have an array `mines` containing the IDs of tiles that contain bombs, and an array `untouched` containing the IDs of tiles the player hasn't clicked yet. After each click, they check whether the player has won by evaluating:

```js
if (untouched.length === mines.length && untouched.every(t => mines.includes(t))) {
  alert("You won!");
}
```

Given the following state:
- `mines = ["tile_3", "tile_7", "tile_15"]`
- `untouched = ["tile_3", "tile_7", "tile_15", "tile_2"]`

The player clicks tile_2, which is not a mine. After the click, `untouched` becomes `["tile_3", "tile_7", "tile_15"]`. The win condition is checked. What is the result?

A. The condition evaluates to `true` and the player sees an alert "You won!" because the arrays have the same length and every untouched tile is in mines.

B. The condition evaluates to `false` because `untouched.every(t => mines.includes(t))` returns `false` since the arrays are not identical.

C. The condition evaluates to `false` because `untouched.length === mines.length` is `false` (lengths differ).

D. The condition evaluates to `true` because `untouched` and `mines` now contain the same elements, but the check is flawed because it only verifies that every untouched tile is in mines, not that every mine is in untouched. However, in this case, the game should not be won because not all mines are accounted for?
options: A. The condition evaluates to `true` and the player sees an alert "You won!" because the arrays have the same length and every untouched tile is in mines.
B. The condition evaluates to `false` because `untouched.every(t => mines.includes(t))` returns `false` since the arrays are not identical.
C. The condition evaluates to `false` because `untouched.length === mines.length` is `false` (lengths differ).

D. The condition evaluates to `true` because `untouched` and `mines` now contain the same elements, but the check is flawed because it only verifies that every untouched tile is in mines, not that every mine is in untouched. However, in this case, the game should not be won because not all mines are accounted for?
answer: A. The condition evaluates to `true` and the player sees an alert "You won!" because the arrays have the same length and every untouched tile is in mines.
explanation: The condition `untouched.length === mines.length && untouched.every(t => mines.includes(t))` checks two things: (1) the number of untouched tiles equals the number of mines, and (2) every untouched tile is a mine. After clicking tile_2, untouched is `["tile_3", "tile_7", "tile_15"]` and mines is `["tile_3", "tile_7", "tile_15"]`. Both have length 3, and every untouched tile (tile_3, tile_7, tile_15) is in mines, so the condition is true. The win condition is triggered, even though the player has not actually won because they still need to clear all non-mine tiles (there are none left, so they have won). The flaw described in option D is that the check does not ensure that every mine is in untouched; but in this case, all mines are untouched, so the win is valid. The condition only checks that the remaining untouched tiles are exactly the mines, which is correct for winning. Option B is wrong because `every` returns true. Option C is wrong because lengths are equal. Option D is misleading because the check is actually correct for this state; the condition ensures the only tiles left are mines, which means the player has cleared all non-mines. The game should be won.

Later, `obj2 = { value: 30 }` reassigns the variable `obj2` to a new object, but this does not affect the reference stored in the array; `arr[1]` still points to the original object. Option A (10) is the value of `obj1.value`. Option C (30) is the value of the new object that `obj2` now points to, but it is not referenced by the array. Option D (undefined) would occur if the array had been mutated to remove the reference or if the property didn't exist, but here it exists and is accessible.

options: A. It compares the arrays by reference and checks that they contain the same elements in the same order.

B. It first checks that the lengths are equal, then uses `every()` to verify that each element in `untouched` appears in `mines`. Since the sets are the same size and every untouched tile is a mine, the sets are identical.
C. It uses `includes()` which performs a deep comparison, so if both arrays have the same elements the condition is true.
D. It works because `untouched` and `mines` are always the same array when the player wins.
answer: B. It first checks that the lengths are equal, then uses `every()` to verify that each element in `untouched` appears in `mines`. Since the sets are the same size and every untouched tile is a mine, the sets are identical.
explanation: Option B is correct. JavaScript does not have built-in array equality, so the developer must manually compare. The condition `untouched.length === mines.length` ensures the arrays have the same size. Then `untouched.every(t => mines.includes(t))` checks that every element in `untouched` is also present in `mines`. Since both arrays have the same length and every element of `untouched` is in `mines`, the two arrays must contain exactly the same elements (though possibly in different order). This logic is described in the course material: 'JavaScript doesn't have a ready-made array comparison operator, so you will need to check whether all the contained values are the same.' Option A is wrong because `===` compares references, not contents. Option C is wrong because `includes()` does not perform deep comparison; it uses strict equality. Option D is wrong because `untouched` and `mines` are separate arrays.

question_text: Alice has just generated a PGP key pair using `gpg --full-generate-key`. She wants to ensure that if she loses her private key, she can still tell others to stop using her public key. Which of the following actions should she take immediately after key generation?
options: A. Upload her public key to a keyserver so others can find it.
B. Generate a revocation certificate and store it offline.
C. Sign her own key with her private key to certify it.
D. Set an expiry date of 2 years for her key.
answer: B. Generate a revocation certificate and store it offline.
explanation: The correct answer is B. According to the course material, 'Generate a revocation certificate immediately and store it offline — this is your "kill switch" if you lose the key.' Option A is something to do later but does not address loss. Option C is unnecessary because the key is already self-signed during generation. Option D is a good practice but does not provide a way to stop others from using a lost key immediately; a revocation certificate is the only way to actively revoke a key when you do not have access to it. Without a revocation certificate, if you lose your private key, you cannot tell the world to stop trusting your public key, and it remains valid until expiry.

question_text: Alice wants to send an encrypted message to Bob using PGP. Which key does she need to encrypt the message?
options: A. Alice's own private key
B. Bob's public key
C. Alice's own public key
D. Bob's private key
answer: B. Bob's public key
explanation: In public-key cryptography, to encrypt a message so that only the intended recipient can read it, you use the recipient's public key. Option A is wrong because Alice's private key is used for signing, not encrypting to someone else. Option C is wrong because using Alice's own public key would encrypt the message to herself, not Bob. Option D is wrong because Bob's private key is secret and never shared; it is used for decryption, not encryption.

question_text: Alice generates a PGP key pair and wants to send an encrypted message to Bob. Which key should she use to encrypt the message?
options: A. Her own private key
B. Her own public key
C. Bob's private key
D. Bob's public key
answer: D. Bob's public key
explanation: In asymmetric cryptography, to ensure that only the intended recipient can read the message, the sender encrypts with the recipient's public key. Only the recipient's private key can decrypt. Option A (her own private key) would allow anyone with her public key to decrypt, not Bob. Option B (her own public key) would allow only she (with her private key) to decrypt, not Bob. Option C (Bob's private key) is impossible because Bob's private key is secret and not shared.

question_text: Alice generates a PGP keypair with `gpg --full-generate-key` and immediately creates a revocation certificate stored on an offline USB. She then encrypts a message to Bob (`gpg --encrypt --recipient bob@example.com message.txt`), but Bob cannot decrypt it. Meanwhile, a malicious actor Mallory obtains Alice's public key from a keyserver. Which of the following best explains why Bob cannot decrypt the message and what Mallory can do?
options: A. Bob cannot decrypt because the message is encrypted with Alice's secret key; Mallory can use Alice's public key to decrypt the message.

B. Bob cannot decrypt because the message is encrypted with Bob's public key, but Bob's secret key may not be available on the system; Mallory can use Alice's public key to encrypt messages to Alice, but cannot decrypt anything.
C. Bob cannot decrypt because the message is encrypted with Alice's public key; Mallory can use Alice's public key to decrypt the message.

D. Bob cannot decrypt because the message is encrypted with Bob's public key, but Bob may have lost his secret key; Mallory can use Bob's public key to encrypt messages to Bob, but cannot decrypt anything.
answer: B. Bob cannot decrypt because the message is encrypted with Bob's public key, but Bob's secret key may not be available on the system; Mallory can use Alice's public key to encrypt messages to Alice, but cannot decrypt anything.
explanation: The correct answer is B. In PGP, encryption uses the recipient's public key, so Alice encrypted the message with Bob's public key. Bob can only decrypt with his corresponding secret key; if that key is not available (e.g., not imported or wrong passphrase), decryption fails. Mallory obtains Alice's public key, which can only be used to encrypt messages to Alice (or verify signatures), not to decrypt anything. Option A is wrong because encryption uses the recipient's public key, not the sender's secret key. Option C is wrong because encryption uses the recipient's public key, not the sender's. Option D correctly identifies the encryption target but incorrectly suggests Mallory can use Bob's public key; Mallory can use any public key to encrypt to that key's owner, but that does not help decrypt the message encrypted to Bob.

question_text:
Alice wants to verify that a Debian ISO she downloaded is authentic and has not been tampered with. She downloads the ISO, the SHA512SUMS file, and the SHA512SUMS.sign file from the official Debian website. She then imports the Debian signing key and runs the following commands:

1. gpg --verify SHA512SUMS.sign SHA512SUMS
2. sha512sum -c SHA512SUMS 2>&1 | grep OK

Command 1 outputs: 'gpg: Good signature from "Debian CD signing key <debian-cd@lists.debian.org>"'
Command 2 outputs: 'debian-12.5.0-amd64-netinst.iso: OK'

Which of the following statements is TRUE about the security guarantees Alice now has?
options: A. Alice knows that the ISO was created and signed by the Debian project, and that the ISO has not been modified since the checksums were published.

B. Alice knows that the ISO was created and signed by the Debian project, but the ISO could have been modified after signing because she only verified the checksum, not the ISO directly against the signature.

C. Alice knows that the ISO has not been modified since the checksums were published, but the signature on SHA512SUMS could be forged if an attacker tricked her into importing a fake Debian signing key.

D. Alice knows that the ISO has not been modified since the checksums were published, and the signature on SHA512SUMS guarantees that the SHA512SUMS file was produced by the Debian project, but Alice cannot be sure who actually signed the ISO because the signature is only on the checksums file, not on the ISO directly.
answer: C. Alice knows that the ISO has not been modified since the checksums were published, but the signature on SHA512SUMS could be forged if an attacker tricked her into importing a fake Debian signing key.
explanation: The correct answer is C. The verification process ensures that the checksums file (SHA512SUMS) is authentic and signed by the Debian project, and that the ISO matches the checksum in that file. Therefore, Alice knows the ISO has not been modified since the checksums were published. However, the security of this process depends on the authenticity of the Debian signing key she imported. If an attacker substituted a fake key, they could sign a fake checksums file with a matching hash for a malicious ISO. Option A is incorrect because Alice cannot be sure the ISO itself was directly signed by Debian; the signature is on the checksums file. Option B is incorrect because the verification of the checksum ensures the ISO matches the signed checksums, so any modification would be detected. Option D is incorrect because while the signature is on the checksums file, that is sufficient to prove the checksums were produced by Debian, and the ISO is verified against those checksums; the concern is about the key's authenticity, not the signature's coverage.

question_text:
A developer wants to use GnuPG to sign a file for distribution. They run the following command:

```sh
gpg --output file.sig --clearsign file.txt
```

Later, they change the content of file.txt and run:

```sh
gpg --verify file.sig
```

What will be the output of the --verify command?
options: A. gpg: Good signature from "Developer" [ultimate]
B. gpg: BAD signature from "Developer" [ultimate]
C. gpg: Signature made ... using RSA key ...
D. gpg: decryption failed: No secret key
answer: B. gpg: BAD signature from "Developer" [ultimate]
explanation: The --clearsign command produces a file that contains the original message followed by a signature. When the file content is changed after signing, the signature no longer matches the modified content. Verification will fail, producing a 'BAD signature' message. Option A is incorrect because a good signature requires the content to be unchanged. Option C is just informational and does not indicate success or failure. Option D is not relevant; --verify does not need a secret key.

question_text: Alice has generated a PGP key pair and uploaded her public key to a keyserver. She receives an encrypted email from Bob, but when she tries to decrypt it, GPG fails with 'decryption failed: No secret key'. Which of the following is the most likely cause of this error?
options: A. Bob encrypted the email using Alice's public key, but Alice's private key has expired.
B. Bob encrypted the email using his own public key instead of Alice's.
C. Alice's public key on the keyserver was replaced by an attacker, so the email was encrypted with a different key.
D. Bob signed the email with his private key but did not encrypt it.
answer: B. Bob encrypted the email using his own public key instead of Alice's.
explanation: The error 'No secret key' means GPG cannot find the private key needed to decrypt the message. If Bob encrypted with his own public key, only his private key can decrypt it, not Alice's. Option A is plausible if the private key expired, but GPG would typically warn about expiration, and the error would be different. Option C is unlikely because if the attacker replaced Alice's key, Bob would have used the attacker's key, and Alice would still have no matching private key, but the error would be similar; however, the most common user error is encrypting with one's own key. Option D describes a signed message, which is not encrypted, so decryption would not be attempted.

Alice wants to send Bob an encrypted and signed message. She writes the message in plaintext, then runs the following command:

gpg --armor --encrypt --recipient bob@example.com message.txt

She then sends the resulting .asc file to Bob. Bob decrypts it successfully but notices there is no signature. What mistake did Alice make?
options: A. She encrypted with her own public key instead of Bob's.
B. She forgot to add the --sign flag before --encrypt.
C. She used --armor which removes signatures.
D. She should have used --clearsign instead of --encrypt.
answer: B. She forgot to add the --sign flag before --encrypt.
explanation: To sign and encrypt in one step, the correct command is gpg --sign --encrypt --armor -r recipient@example.com message.txt. Option A is incorrect because if she encrypted with her own public key, only she could decrypt, not Bob. Option C is wrong because --armor only changes output format to ASCII-armored base64; it does not affect signatures. Option D is wrong because --clearsign only signs the message (without encryption), so it would not provide confidentiality.

question_text: Alice wants to send Bob an encrypted message that also proves she wrote it. She correctly signs the message with her private key and encrypts the result with Bob's public key. Which of the following best describes the security properties of the final ciphertext?
options: A. Only Bob can decrypt the message, and anyone with Bob's public key can verify Alice's signature.
B. Only Bob can decrypt the message, and only Bob can verify Alice's signature.
C. Anyone with Alice's public key can decrypt the message, and only Bob can verify Alice's signature.
D. Only Bob can decrypt the message, and anyone with Alice's public key can verify Alice's signature.
answer: D. Only Bob can decrypt the message, and anyone with Alice's public key can verify Alice's signature.
explanation: The correct answer is D. In PGP, encryption is done with the recipient's public key, so only Bob (who holds the corresponding private key) can decrypt. Signing is done with the sender's private key, so anyone with Alice's public key can verify the signature. Option A is incorrect because Bob's public key does not verify Alice's signature—Alice's public key does. Option B is incorrect because signature verification does not require Bob's private key. Option C is incorrect because decryption requires Bob's private key, not Alice's public key.

question_text: Alice wants to send Bob an encrypted email that also proves she wrote it. She first signs the message with her private key, then encrypts the result with Bob's public key. Bob receives the ciphertext and decrypts it with his private key, obtaining a signed message. Which of the following best describes the security properties of this process?
options: A. The encrypted email provides both confidentiality and authentication because only Bob can decrypt it and Alice's signature can be verified by anyone with her public key.
B. The encrypted email provides confidentiality but not authentication because anyone with Bob's public key could have encrypted the message; the signature is only useful if Bob also verifies it.
C. The encrypted email provides authentication but not confidentiality because Alice's signature is embedded in the ciphertext and can be extracted by any third party.
D. The encrypted email provides neither confidentiality nor authentication because the signature and encryption keys are the same.
answer: B. The encrypted email provides confidentiality but not authentication because anyone with Bob's public key could have encrypted the message; the signature is only useful if Bob also verifies it.
explanation: The correct answer is B. Encryption with Bob's public key ensures that only Bob (who holds the corresponding private key) can decrypt the message, thus providing confidentiality. However, authentication (proof of authorship) is not automatically achieved by encryption because anyone with Bob's public key could have encrypted a message to Bob. The signature must be verified by Bob using Alice's public key to confirm that the message originated from Alice. Option A is incorrect because it incorrectly states that authentication is provided by the encryption process; the signature must be verified separately. Option C is incorrect because the ciphertext is encrypted and the signature is inside the encryption, so a third party cannot extract the signature without Bob's private key. Option D is incorrect because signature and encryption keys are different (Alice's private key for signing, Bob's public key for encryption); the operations use separate keys.

question_text:
Alice wants to send Bob an encrypted email that also proves she wrote it. She writes a message.txt file and then runs the following GPG command:

  gpg --armor --encrypt --sign -r bob@example.com message.txt

What is the primary security property that the --sign flag adds compared to using only --encrypt?
options: A. It ensures the message cannot be read by anyone except Bob, because signing also encrypts the message with Alice's public key.
B. It provides non-repudiation by allowing Bob to verify that the message was signed by Alice's private key, proving authorship.
C. It compresses the message to reduce its size, making the encrypted output smaller than without --sign.
D. It allows the message to be decrypted by anyone who has Alice's public key, which is necessary for decryption.
answer: B. It provides non-repudiation by allowing Bob to verify that the message was signed by Alice's private key, proving authorship.
explanation: The correct answer is B. In GPG, the --sign flag uses Alice's private key to create a digital signature on the message. This signature can be verified by anyone with Alice's public key, providing proof that the message was indeed signed by Alice and has not been tampered with. This property is called non-repudiation. Option A is incorrect because the encryption is performed using the recipient's public key, not the sender's. Signing does not encrypt; it only signs. Option C is incorrect because signing does not compress the message; it adds a signature, making the output slightly larger, not smaller. Option D is incorrect because decryption requires the recipient's private key, not the sender's public key.

question_text: Alice generates a new PGP keypair, immediately creates a revocation certificate, and stores it on a USB drive. She then publishes her public key to a keyserver. Six months later, Alice loses her laptop containing her private key. Which of the following is the best way for Alice to prevent people from trusting her compromised key?
options: A. Upload the revocation certificate to the keyserver to revoke the key.
B. Use gpg --edit-key to change the key's expiry date to today, then re-upload.
C. Generate a new keypair and ask everyone to delete the old one.
D. Nothing; the key cannot be revoked because only the private key can sign a revocation certificate.
answer: A. Upload the revocation certificate to the keyserver to revoke the key.
explanation:
The correct answer is A. A revocation certificate is a signed statement (by the original owner) that marks a public key as no longer valid. Since Alice generated and stored the revocation certificate when she first created her key (as recommended in the course material: 'Generate a revocation certificate immediately and store it offline'), she can use it even after losing her private key. Uploading it to the keyserver will propagate the revocation, informing others that the key should not be trusted.

Option B is incorrect because changing the expiry date requires access to the private key (via gpg --edit-key), which Alice has lost. Option C is not practical; even if Alice generates a new keypair, the old one remains on keyservers and may still be used by others. Option D is false because a revocation certificate, generated ahead of time, does not require the private key to use — it is a pre-signed document.
